coaching.coaching: Pin down distinguishable test cases for each AC property with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: distinguishable test cases for each AC property

coaching.coaching: Plan how calibration threshold verification strategy will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: calibration threshold verification strategy

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the calibration child by alias or ticket id in the Dependencies section so the third required dependency is trackable and verifiable.
evidence: Dependencies section: 'Depends on the criterion, coaching, and calibration children so the documentation describes the final validated behavior rather than an intermediate design.'

Approach: 'the balanced eight-case eval path with exact release thresholds recall: 1.0, false_accept: 0.0, agreement: 1.0, per-case stability >= 0.6666666667, plus expected-vs-observed fire/no-fire kappa >= 0.70'
immune-floury-toad found as criterion child; truthful-specified-silverfish found as coaching child; no third ticket found or named
ABSENCE: no alias or ticket id for a calibration child appears anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The Dependencies section says 'Depends on the criterion, coaching, and calibration children' but names only two children by alias: `immune-floury-toad` (criterion/finder) and `truthful-specified-silverfish` (coaching). The third 'calibration child' — whose validated calibration thresholds the subsection must document — has no alias, ticket id, or any other identifier anywhere in this plan, making it impossible to verify the dependency exists or is resolved before this plan executes.
findings.location: Dependencies section; Approach paragraph 'the balanced eight-case eval path with exact release thresholds'
scenarios: The calibration child has not landed when this ticket is claimed; the author documents placeholder thresholds that don't match the final validated values, violating the stated dependency rationale.
The calibration child ticket doesn't exist or was never created, making the documented thresholds unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the calibration child's alias/ticket id to the Dependencies section, e.g. 'Depends on `immune-floury-toad` (criterion activation), `truthful-specified-silverfish` (coaching move), and `<calibration-child-alias>` (eval calibration) so the documentation describes the final validated behavior.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the calibration child ticket by alias or id in the Dependencies section and confirm that its eval results yield exactly `recall: 1.0`, `false_accept: 0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, `stability >= 0.6666666667`, `kappa >= 0.70` before hardcoding those literals into the documentation and the structural test.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'the balanced eight-case eval path with exact release thresholds `recall: 1.0`, `false_accept: 0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, per-case `stability >= 0.6666666667`, plus expected-vs-observed fire/no-fire `kappa >= 0.70`'
Dependencies section: 'Depends on the criterion, coaching, and calibration children' — calibration child has no alias or ticket id
src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/ directory listing: no plan-review-project-portability.eval.yaml exists
.rebar/ directory listing: no evals/ subdirectory exists — calibration fixture for project.portability not yet authored
eval.py lines 411-421: a project criterion's calibration fixture resolves to `.rebar/evals/plan-review-project-portability.eval.yaml`, which is absent
findings.finding: The plan hardcodes five calibration thresholds (`recall: 1.0`, `false_accept: 0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, `stability >= 0.6666666667`, `kappa >= 0.70`) as exact documentation literals, verified by a structural test (`-k calibration_example`), but no calibration eval fixture exists in the repo (no `.rebar/evals/plan-review-project-portability.eval.yaml` and no entry in `src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/`) and the 'calibration child' that would generate these values is never named by ticket id or alias in the plan.
findings.location: Approach section (calibration thresholds paragraph); AC: 'The subsection contains all five exact live calibration thresholds. Prove with -k calibration_example.'
scenarios: The calibration child (unidentified) produces thresholds differing from the five literals; the structural test `-k calibration_example` then fails against the documented thresholds on merge.
An implementer documents the hardcoded thresholds before the calibration child runs, shipping documentation that contradicts the actual eval results and locking a reviewer into wrong expectations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the calibration child's ticket alias to the Dependencies section (e.g. 'Depends on immune-floury-toad, truthful-specified-silverfish, and <calibration-child-alias>'). State explicitly that the five threshold values in the Approach are to be filled in from the calibration child's output once it lands, not hardcoded in advance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split ACs 1 and 2 onto distinct test selectors (e.g., `-k project_move_schema` for the schema shape and `-k project_move_applies_when` for the three-case semantics) so each property has an independently falsifiable proof.
evidence: AC 1 asserts schema format; AC 2 asserts applies_when behavioral semantics — distinct properties sharing one test selector
findings.finding: ACs 1 and 2 both declare `-k project_move_schema` as their sole proof mechanism, yet they assert two logically distinct properties: (1) the schema shape `{move_id: {name, template, applies_when?}}` and (2) the three-case `applies_when` semantics (absent / empty / non-empty). A single test node that passes could silently omit verification of one property, making one AC unverifiably satisfied.
scenarios: Implementer writes a test_project_move_schema that checks the schema shape but omits the three-case applies_when semantics; both ACs show green without the second property being verified.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the applies_when sub-test `test_project_move_applies_when` and update AC 2's proof line to `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_docs.py -k project_move_applies_when`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign distinct `-k` node names to AC 1 and AC 2 (e.g. `-k project_move_schema_shape` for the `{name, template, applies_when?}` check and `-k project_move_applies_when_semantics` for the absent/empty/non-empty rules), so each AC is independently provable.
evidence: AC 1: 'The existing project-extension paragraph documents `{move_id: {name, template, applies_when?}}`. Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_docs.py -k project_move_schema`.'

AC 2: 'The existing project-extension paragraph defines absent, empty, plus non-empty `applies_when` behavior. Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_docs.py -k project_move_schema`.'

These are distinct assertions — schema shape vs. behavioral semantics — mapped to the same test node name, creating ambiguity about whether one test or two will be authored and what a test failure means.
findings.finding: ACs 1 and 2 share the identical test node name `-k project_move_schema`, yet they assert two structurally distinct properties: (1) the schema shape `{move_id: {name, template, applies_when?}}` is documented, and (2) the behavioral semantics for absent, empty, and non-empty `applies_when` are defined. A single `-k project_move_schema` selector will run one test function; if both properties are not co-located inside that one function, one property goes unproven.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 1–2: both reference '-k project_move_schema'
scenarios: An implementer writes `test_project_move_schema` to check the schema string but omits the behavioral semantics assertions; AC 2 passes vacuously because pytest -k finds the same node.
An implementer writes two functions both containing 'project_move_schema' in their name and `-k project_move_schema` runs both — but the plan gives no indication this was intended.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into: AC 1 proves with `-k project_move_schema_shape`; AC 2 proves with `-k project_move_applies_when_semantics`. Add a note in the test strategy that these are two assertions within separate test functions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the five calibration thresholds in a single authoritative location (e.g., a constants block in the test file or a YAML/TOML config the test imports) so that the doc and the test both derive from one source, preventing silent drift on recalibration.
evidence: 'the balanced eight-case eval path with exact release thresholds `recall: 1.0`, `false_accept: 0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, per-case `stability >= 0.6666666667`, plus expected-vs-observed fire/no-fire `kappa >= 0.70`'
AC: 'The subsection contains all five exact live calibration thresholds. Prove with ... -k calibration_example'
No plan text names a single authoritative source file from which both the doc and test derive these values
findings.finding: Calibration thresholds (`recall: 1.0`, `false_accept: 0.0`, `agreement: 1.0`, `stability >= 0.6666666667`, `kappa >= 0.70`) are hardcoded as literals in the documentation AND verified by exact string-match in the structural test, with no single configurable source of truth. If a recalibration child updates these values, both `docs/plan-review-gate.md` and `tests/unit/test_project_portability_docs.py` must be manually updated in lockstep with no mechanical guard against drift.
scenarios: A recalibration exercise updates `kappa >= 0.70` to `kappa >= 0.75`; the doc is updated but the test still asserts the old literal and silently fails CI until someone notices
A contributor updates the test constant but forgets the doc, leaving the documentation showing stale thresholds
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_project_portability_docs.py`, declare the five threshold values as module-level constants (e.g., `RECALL_THRESHOLD = '1.0'`). The test asserts the doc contains these constants' string representations; the doc section cross-references the same values. A comment in both artifacts points to the constants block as the authoritative source.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
